stem: To approximate \( \sqrt{[[N]]} \), Newton's method applies the iteration \( x_{k+1} = x_k - \dfrac{x_k^{2}-[[N]]}{2x_k} \), starting from \( x_0 = [[x0]] \). What is \( x_{[[n]]} \)?
solution: Applying \( x_{k+1}=x_k-\dfrac{x_k^{2}-[[N]]}{2x_k} \) starting from \( x_0=[[x0]] \), \( [[n]] \) iteration(s) give \( x_{[[n]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to average \( x_0 \) with \( N/x_0 \) using the correct weighting (this uses equal weight 1, not the Newton update).
This forgets to divide the correction term \( x_0^2-N \) by \( 2x_0 \).
This reports \( N/x_0 \) alone rather than the full Newton update.
This leaves \( x \) unchanged from the starting value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: To approximate the cube root of [[N]], Newton's method applies the iteration \( x_{k+1} = x_k - \dfrac{x_k^{3}-[[N]]}{3x_k^{2}} \), starting from \( x_0 = [[x0]] \). What is \( x_{[[n]]} \)?
solution: Here the derivative is \( 3x_k^{2} \); applying the iteration \( [[n]] \) time(s) from \( x_0=[[x0]] \) gives \( x_{[[n]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This misweights the average of \( x_0 \) and \( N/x_0^{2} \) (uses the square-root update's weighting instead of the cube-root one).
This forgets to divide the correction term by \( 3x_0^{2} \).
This reports \( N/x_0^{2} \) alone rather than the full Newton update.
This leaves \( x \) unchanged from the starting value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Newton's method for computing \( \dfrac{1}{[[N]]} \) without dividing at each step uses the iteration \( x_{k+1} = x_k(2 - [[N]]x_k) \), starting from \( x_0 = [[x0]] \). What is \( x_{[[n]]} \)?
solution: Applying \( x_{k+1}=x_k(2-[[N]]x_k) \) \( [[n]] \) time(s) from \( x_0=[[x0]] \) gives \( x_{[[n]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This has the wrong sign inside the parentheses.
This forgets to multiply by \( x_0 \) at all.
This uses 1 instead of 2 as the constant in the update rule.
This jumps straight to the true reciprocal, ignoring the finite number of iterations actually applied.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(x) = [[a]]x^{2} + ([[b]])x + ([[c]]) \). Newton's method updates an estimate via \( x_{1} = x_0 - \dfrac{f(x_0)}{f'(x_0)} \). Starting from \( x_0 = [[x0]] \), what is \( x_1 \)?
distractor_rationales: This forgets to divide the correction \( f(x_0) \) by \( f'(x_0) \).
This has the wrong sign on the correction term.
This leaves \( x \) unchanged from the starting value.
This flips the sign of the starting point instead of applying Newton's update.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

distractor_rationales: This forgets to divide the correction \( f(x_0) \) by \( f'(x_0) \).
This has the wrong sign on the correction term.
This leaves \( x \) unchanged from the starting value.
This flips the sign of the starting point instead of applying Newton's update.
